subject: Chemistry
title: The First 33 Elements
description: A micro-lesson that makes The First 33 Elements usable.

hook.text: By 1800, chemists had found 33 elements. Some were known since antiquity—gold, silver, copper, iron.

buildup.text: Ancient peoples found metals in ores and sulfur near volcanoes. But they didn't call them elements.

discovery.text: Lavoisier published the first real list of elements in 1789. He listed 33, including light and heat.

twist.text: Light and heat aren't elements. Lavoisier was wrong about those but right about almost everything else.

climax.text: His list inspired a race. Over the next century, chemists would triple the number of known elements.

punchline.text: 33 elements on one list. The starting gun for element hunting.

quiz.question: What mistake did Lavoisier make in his first element list?
options: He included light and heat as elements
He listed water as an element
